macro: The Fed held its policy rate at 3.50%-3.75% at the June 16-17 FOMC meeting under new Chair Kevin Warsh, and June's jobs report has since reinforced the case for another hold: nonfarm payrolls rose just 57,000, well below the 115,000 consensus, while April and May were revised down a combined 74,000. The unemployment rate still dipped to 4.2%, but only because labor-force participation fell to 61.5%, its lowest since March 2021, masking real softening beneath the headline number. Markets have pushed the probability of a hold at the July 28-29 meeting to 75.6% on CME FedWatch, even though nine of 18 officials' own projections still pencil in a hike before year-end and core PCE remains near 3.3%. The single most important event this week: Wednesday's FOMC minutes from the June meeting, the first released under Warsh, which should clarify how much weight the committee is putting on the labor-market wobble versus still-elevated inflation.
btc_status: Bitcoin trades near $62,900, just above its roughly $62,457 200-week moving average — a level that flipped from resistance back to support after a recent 21-month-low scare — while the Fear & Greed Index sits at 24 (Extreme Fear). The level to watch is that $62,457 floor: a close back below it opens a path toward $59,000, while reclaiming the $65,500-$67,180 resistance cluster would mark a firmer stabilization.

AVOID TODAY
AMD — Down 5% to $511.67 on July 2 as chip stocks gave back gains after an 80%-plus first-half rally; the move looks like sector-wide profit-taking rather than a company-specific problem, but it argues against chasing strength here
energy: WTI crude trades near $68.78 a barrel, little changed on the day, while Brent holds around $72. The EIA's Weekly Petroleum Status Report for the week ended June 26 (released July 1) showed commercial crude stocks fell 3.8 million barrels to 408.4 million barrels, about 7% below the five-year seasonal average. Supply-risk premia have eased as Saudi Arabia, Russia and five other OPEC+ members agreed to raise output by a further 188,000 barrels a day next month, a signal the group sees Middle East conditions stabilizing.
vix_and_dollar: The VIX's most recent close was 15.81, down 2.11%, comfortably inside the 15-20 'normal' band heading into the holiday-shortened week. The Dollar Index (DXY) sits near 101.1, having posted its largest weekly decline since April after June payrolls badly missed forecasts and traders scaled back Fed rate-hike bets — a combination of calm equity volatility and a softer dollar that points to reduced, not eliminated, hedging demand.
mortgage_rate: The 30-year fixed mortgage rate averaged 6.43% for the week ended July 2, 2026, per Freddie Mac's Primary Mortgage Market Survey — a seven-week low, down from 6.49% the prior week. NAR's most recent existing-home sales data (May) showed a 4.5-month supply and a housing affordability index of 105.6, up sharply from 97.5 a year ago, so easing rates are landing on top of a market that's already gotten meaningfully more affordable.
personal_finance_deep_dive: Bankrate's 2026 Emergency Savings Report found 24% of Americans have no emergency savings at all, and 47% couldn't cover a $1,000 surprise expense out of savings. More than half, 54%, say inflation is actively causing them to save less, and 29% say their credit card balance now exceeds their emergency fund — a reversal of the traditional order of financial priorities. The pattern points to a household sector still running down its cash buffers even as headline employment data, before June's miss, looked solid, leaving lower- and middle-income earners most exposed to a single unexpected expense turning into new debt.
wealth_building_deep_dive: Vanguard's 25th annual How America Saves report found the average 401(k) balance hit a record $167,970 at year-end 2025, up 13% in a single year, while the median balance rose 16% to $44,115. The gain was driven as much by plan design as by markets: auto-enrollment now covers 61% of Vanguard-administered plans, up from 34% in 2013, and roughly 31% of participants had their deferral rate bumped up automatically through auto-escalation last year. The concrete takeaway for savers behind the median: check whether your plan offers auto-escalation and turn it on — a 1-point-a-year increase, often timed to a raise, closes the gap without ever requiring a manual decision to save more.
notable: Progress Software (PRGS) jumped 19.3% on July 1 after a Q2 EPS and revenue beat and a raised FY26 guidance range of $6.09-$6.21 per share (GuruFocus, TipRanks, July 1, 2026).

Chip stocks gave back ground on July 2 as AMD fell 5% to $511.67 and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) dropped 6% to $561.49, profit-taking after the group's 80%-plus first-half rally (24/7 Wall St., July 2, 2026).
Silver jumped roughly 3% to open the week near $62.92/oz after Thursday's weak jobs report reduced Fed rate-hike expectations (Yahoo Finance, July 6, 2026).
